quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "London dispersion forces"?

options.text: A model stating reactions occur when molecules collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation
Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area
ΔH for any reaction equals the sum of ΔH values for the steps into which the reaction can be divided, regardless of path
Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest

quiz_questions.explanation: "London dispersion forces" is defined as: Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Dipole-dipole interactions"?

options.text: Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)
The arrangement of only the atoms (bonding pairs) around the central atom (e.g., bent for 2 bonding + 2 lone pairs)
Attractions between the positive end of one polar molecule and the negative end of another; present in polar molecules
The equilibrium distance between two bonded nuclei; decreases as bond order increases: triple < double < single

quiz_questions.explanation: "Dipole-dipole interactions" is defined as: Attractions between the positive end of one polar molecule and the negative end of another; present in polar molecules. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hydrogen bonding"?

options.text: Two or more reactants combine to form one product: A + B → AB (e.g., 2Na + Cl₂ → 2NaCl)
The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea
Separation based on density differences using rapid spinning; denser particles settle to the bottom (pellet), lighter components remain in solution (supernatant)
A strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F atom

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hydrogen bonding" is defined as: A strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F atom. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Ion-dipole force"?

options.text: Most reactions are reversible and reach equilibrium; very few go to completion (irreversible) unless products are removed from the system
The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water
Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases
π = iMRT; the pressure needed to prevent osmosis across a semipermeable membrane; used to determine molar mass of large molecules

quiz_questions.explanation: "Ion-dipole force" is defined as: The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Polarizability"?

options.text: A weak acid or base that changes color at a specific pH range; chosen so its color change range includes the equivalence point pH (e.g., phenolphthalein, pH 8.2–10, ideal for weak acid–strong base titrations; bromothymol blue, pH 6.0–7.6, for...
The ease with which an electron cloud can be distorted; increases with atomic/molecular size and number of electrons
A tube filled with inert electrolyte (KNO₃, KCl) connecting two half-cells; allows ion migration to maintain electrical neutrality
The change in concentration over a finite time interval: Δ[A]/Δt; decreases over time as reactants are consumed

quiz_questions.explanation: "Polarizability" is defined as: The ease with which an electron cloud can be distorted; increases with atomic/molecular size and number of electrons. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Temporary dipole-induced dipole attractions present in ALL molecules; strength increases with molar mass, polarizability, and surface area"?

options.text: Catalyst effect
Homogeneous catalyst
London dispersion forces
Ka (acid dissociation constant)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "London dispersion forces".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Attractions between the positive end of one polar molecule and the negative end of another; present in polar molecules"?

options.text: Spectator ion
ΔS prediction rules
Catalyst
Dipole-dipole interactions

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Dipole-dipole interactions".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F atom"?

options.text: Spontaneous process
Hydrogen bonding
Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)
End point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hydrogen bonding".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water"?

options.text: Viscosity
Ion-dipole force
Homogeneous catalyst
Kw (autoionization constant)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Ion-dipole force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The ease with which an electron cloud can be distorted; increases with atomic/molecular size and number of electrons"?

options.text: Catalyst
Limiting reagent
Kp vs. Kc
Polarizability

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Polarizability".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Intermolecular Forces is accurate?

options.text: Energy exists as kinetic energy (motion) and potential energy (position/composition).
Strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HClO₄) and strong bases (group 1/2 hydroxides) dissociate completely.
Strong acid–strong base titrations have equivalence point at pH 7.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Intermolecular Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Intermolecular Forces?

options.text: Crystalline solids have ordered lattice structures (ionic, molecular, covalent network, metallic), while amorphous solids (glass) lack long-range order.
Molecular Geometry is predicted by VSEPR theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion): electron groups around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion.
Percent yield (actual/theoretical × 100%) measures reaction efficiency.

In order of increasing strength: London dispersion forces (LDFs, present in all molecules, increase with molar mass and surface area), dipole-dipole interactions (polar molecules), and hydrogen bonds (H bonded to N, O, or F interacting with lone...

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Intermolecular Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Intermolecular Forces?

options.text: Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
Batteries and Corrosion applies electrochemistry to practical applications.
Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Intermolecular Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Intermolecular Forces?

options.text: Crystalline solids have ordered lattice structures (ionic, molecular, covalent network, metallic), while amorphous solids (glass) lack long-range order.
Intermolecular Forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules that determine physical properties like boiling point, viscosity, and surface tension.

This delocalized electron model explains metallic properties: electrical conductivity (electrons flow freely), thermal conductivity (electrons transfer kinetic energy), malleability and ductility (layers of cations slide without breaking bonds),...
Lewis structures show bonding and lone pairs, with formal charge minimization guiding the best structure.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Intermolecular Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Intermolecular Forces?

options.text: Stoichiometry is the quantitative study of reactants and products in chemical reactions based on the mole concept.
Collision Theory explains reaction rates at the molecular level: for a reaction to occur, molecules must (1) collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy, Ea) and (2) have proper orientation.

In order of increasing strength: London dispersion forces (LDFs, present in all molecules, increase with molar mass and surface area), dipole-dipole interactions (polar molecules), and hydrogen bonds (H bonded to N, O, or F interacting with lone...
Q (reaction quotient) compared to K predicts the direction a reaction will shift.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Intermolecular Forces as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Intermolecular Forces?

options.text: Breathalyzer devices using electrochemical oxidation of ethanol to acetic acid, generating a current proportional to blood alcohol concentration
Dissolution of ammonium nitrate: endothermic (ΔH > 0) but spontaneous because the large increase in entropy (ΔS >> 0) from ions dispersing in solution makes ΔG < 0
Water's anomalously high boiling point (100°C vs. H₂S at -60°C) due to extensive hydrogen bonding networks between molecules
Refrigerators and air conditioners: external work (electricity) is required to decrease entropy locally (cool the interior) while increasing total universal entropy

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Intermolecular Forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Intermolecular Forces?

options.text: Combustion of hydrocarbons in engines: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O releasing energy to power vehicles; incomplete combustion produces toxic CO
Nitrogen dioxide equilibrium (2NO₂ ⇌ N₂O₄): the brown-to-colorless color change visually demonstrates equilibrium shifting with temperature changes
Gecko adhesion: billions of tiny setae on gecko feet exploit London dispersion forces to adhere to surfaces, inspiring synthetic 'gecko tape' technologies
Road salt (NaCl) lowering the freezing point of water to prevent ice formation on roads—effective down to about -9°C (ΔTf = iKfm, i = 2 for NaCl)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Intermolecular Forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Intermolecular Forces applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Soap and detergent action: amphipathic molecules with ionic heads (ion-dipole with water) and nonpolar tails (LDF with grease) bridging oil and water
Vinegar concentration determination: titrating with standardized NaOH; the acetic acid content must be 4-8% by mass for commercial vinegar
Biological research: phosphate buffer (H₂PO₄⁻/HPO₄²⁻, pKa = 7.20) commonly used in cell culture media to maintain physiological pH
Corrosion of iron (rusting): 4Fe + 3O₂ + 6H₂O → 4Fe(OH)₃ → 2Fe₂O₃·3H₂O; prevented by galvanizing (Zn coating) or painting

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Intermolecular Forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Intermolecular Forces?

options.text: Table salt (NaCl) as a classic ionic compound: Na donates one electron to Cl, forming Na⁺Cl⁻ with a crystal lattice structure and high melting point (801°C)
Water's anomalously high boiling point (100°C vs. H₂S at -60°C) due to extensive hydrogen bonding networks between molecules
Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)
Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Intermolecular Forces.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Intermolecular Forces in practice?

options.text: Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content
Carbonated beverages: dissolved CO₂ forms carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), a diprotic acid giving soft drinks their tangy taste (pH ~3-4)
Solar water heating systems using water's high specific heat capacity to absorb and store thermal energy efficiently
Gecko adhesion: billions of tiny setae on gecko feet exploit London dispersion forces to adhere to surfaces, inspiring synthetic 'gecko tape' technologies

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Intermolecular Forces.
